text: The surgeon uses an applied medical key files trocar to enter the right retrorectus space, sweeping fibril fatty areolar tissue away from the posterior sheath. Two robotic ports are placed under direct visualization, and the initial five-millimeter port is upsized to an eight-millimeter robotic port. The epigastric eight-millimeter robotic port is upsized to twelve millimeters for heavyweight mesh placement. Dissection proceeds towards the linea semilinare, revealing a large neurovascular bundle. The surgeon dissects the entire retrorectus space before starting the TAR by incising the posterior lamella of the internal oblique and dividing a medial neurovascular bundle. The posterior lamella of the internal oblique is divided, followed by the transverse abdominus muscle, to access the pre-transverse salus fascia plane.

The surgeon attempts to separate the transversus abdominis muscle from the transverse salus fascia, inadvertently creating a small hole in the peritoneum. The CO₂ pressure equalizes as the surgeon pinches off the hole to maintain the workspace. The TAR procedure continues caudally, with the muscle transitioning to a more aponeurotic form, facilitating separation from the fascia using the ETAP technique.

The surgeon performs a complete transverse abdominus release, dissecting towards the hernia sac. The dissection progresses under the diaphragm and below the hernia sac in the right groin. The aponeurotic portion of the posterior sheath is divided medial to the linea semilunaris, near the hernia sac. Visible fat is located below the hernia sac as the dissection approaches the right lower quadrant.

The assistant applies pressure on the hernia while the surgeon flips the scope to work around the hernia neck. The surgeon attempts to stay outside the hernia sac, aiming to reduce the sac and its contents without entering the abdominal cavity. The surgeon struggles to find a plane between the hernia sac and surrounding structures, noting the presence of muscle and a thin piece of peritoneum. Eventually, the surgeon enters the hernia sac, accessing the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon attempts to peel the hernia sac away from the hernia walls, revealing its extremely thin structure. The hernia contains pericolonic fat and portions of the right colon, which are reduced while the patient is in the lateral decubitus position. The colon is tethered within the sac, but the adhesions are minimal and easily removed using robotic instruments. Visible muscle striations are seen in the background as the surgeon snips adhesive bands and divides the neck of the hernia, continuing lateral dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects the lateral portion of the hernia sac, staying close to the peritoneum. The left hand retracts the posterior perirenal fascia, revealing two distinct tissue layers. The lateral conal fascia is visible, with no discrete defect observed, indicating intact layers. The goal is to achieve extensive overlap for mesh placement without securing it.

The surgeon navigates through scar tissue, maintaining the dissection within the established plane between the posterior perirenal fascia and lateral conal fascia. The patient is positioned in straight lateral decubitus, aiding in the medial rotation of the peritoneum and colon. The dissection progresses into the pre-transverse salus fascia plane in the upper abdomen, revealing areolar tissue near the kidney area.

The psoas muscle becomes visible as the surgeon carefully dissects anterior to it, preserving the psoas fascia to avoid injuring nearby nerves such as the genitofemoral, lateral femoral cutaneous, iliohypogastric, and ilioinguinal nerves. The area is heavily scarred from a previous partial nephrectomy, with the kidney being retracted medially. A Weck clip from the nephrectomy is encountered, and the wristed instrument aids in maneuvering around the kidney amidst the dense scar tissue.

The surgeon identifies a white Weck clip and observes the lateral portion of the incisional hernia. Despite being very lateral, the surgeon decides to extend the dissection for better overlap with a heavyweight mesh due to the patient's high BMI. A small peritoneal hole is noted near the lateral portion of the liver, left in place. The main defect measures six centimeters cranially and eight centimeters laterally, with visible weakness but not a true hernia.

The surgeon closes the peritoneal hole at the neck of the hernia sac using a 3-0 suture, laying out the OV lock suture to manage slack. The main defect is visible, lacking a significant hernia sac. The surgeon decreases pneumoperitoneum to 8 mmHg to prevent muscle tears, noting the defect spans two muscle layers, likely the transverse abdominis and internal oblique. The left hand braces the tissue while the right hand tightens the suture.

The surgeon identifies a large hole beneath the external oblique muscle and places a 5mm port under direct vision into the space. A 19 French JP drain is threaded through the port into the cavity to prevent seroma formation. The closure involves muscle-to-muscle suturing with heavy weight mesh for reinforcement, as there is some tension centrally but not medially or laterally. A second row of sutures is applied to reduce tension on the initial suture line.

The surgeon examines the space for mesh placement, identifying anatomical landmarks such as the anterior superior iliac spine and iliac crest. Instruments are positioned between the peritoneum and lateral conal fascia, and between the transverse salus and transverse abdominus. The space measures approximately 25 cm long by 26 cm wide. The surgeon prepares to place heavyweight mesh, upsizing the optical entry port to 12 mm to accommodate the mesh, and performs additional dissection for caudal overlap.

The surgeon tucks the posterior lateral edge of the heavyweight mesh into the groove adjacent to the psoas muscle, ensuring a lateral overlap of approximately ten centimeters. The mesh is positioned carefully to avoid nerve injury, using a suture technique for securing long mesh pieces.
